areasOfOperation.title: Preflight Preparation
areasOfOperation.groundingNotes: ARROW documents must be on board: Airworthiness certificate, Registration, Radio station license (international only), Operating limitations (AFM/POH), Weight and balance. Any mention of ARROW or AROW is correct for these. Pilot must carry: pilot certificate, medical certificate, government-issued photo ID (14 CFR 61.3).

tasks.title: Pilot Qualifications
tasks.groundingNotes: Private pilot privileges (14 CFR 61.113): may act as PIC of aircraft for personal use; may carry passengers; may not act as PIC for compensation or hire except narrow exceptions (14 CFR 61.113(b)-(h)). Medical: third-class required; valid 60 months if under 40, 24 months if 40 or older (BasicMed: 48 months, different requirements). Recent flight experience to carry passengers: 3 takeoffs and landings within 90 days in the same category/class; night passenger currency requires those 3 to be full-stop after sunset (14 CFR 61.57). Student solo endorsements: must be current (90-day or as specified by CFI). Logbook not required on person but endorsements must be accessible. Note that 90 days is not '3 months', and other time durations must be exactly as stated in the regulation and not a colloquial-seeming equivalent.

tasks.title: Airworthiness Requirements
tasks.groundingNotes: PIC responsible for determining aircraft airworthiness before flight (14 CFR 91.7). Airworthiness certificate must be displayed (14 CFR 91.203). Annual inspection required every 12 calendar months (14 CFR 91.409). 100-hour inspection required if aircraft operated for hire or flight instruction for hire. ADs (Airworthiness Directives) must be complied with — recurring ADs tracked in maintenance records. Inoperative equipment: use 14 CFR 91.213 — if no MEL, equipment may be inoperative if (1) not required by 14 CFR 91.205 or flight rules, (2) not part of a required inspection item, (3) placarded INOP, (4) maintenance performed per 14 CFR part 43 if needed. AVIATES checklist for required VFR day instruments: Altimeter, VSI, Tach, Airspeed, Temperature (oil), Engine instruments (oil pressure), Fuel gauges, Magnetic compass.

tasks.title: Weather Information
tasks.groundingNotes: VFR minimums in Class G below 1200 ft AGL day: 1 SM visibility, clear of clouds. Class G below 1200 ft AGL night: 3 SM, 500 below/1000 above/2000 horizontal. Class E: 3 SM, 500 below/1000 above/2000 horizontal. Class B: 3 SM, clear of clouds. Class C/D: 3 SM, 500 below/1000 above/2000 horizontal. Weather products: METARs (current), TAFs (forecast, 24-30 hrs), PIREPs (pilot reports), AIRMETs (G=turbulence/icing/mtn wave, S=IFR, T=thunderstorms for light aircraft), SIGMETs (severe/extreme conditions). Standard briefing from 1800wxbrief.com or Leidos. Wind shear on approach: sudden airspeed loss = windshear; recovery: full power, arrest descent rate. Fuel reserves: day VFR 30 minutes at normal cruise; night VFR 45 minutes (14 CFR 91.151).

tasks.title: Cross-Country Flight Planning
tasks.groundingNotes: True course → True heading (apply wind correction angle) → Magnetic heading (apply variation, east is least, west is best) → Compass heading (apply deviation). VFR cruising altitudes above 3000 ft AGL: 0-179° magnetic = odd thousands + 500 ft; 180-359° = even thousands + 500 ft (14 CFR 91.159). Flight plan filed with FSS — not required for VFR but recommended. Cross-country requires landing at point more than 50 NM from departure. Sectional scale 1:500,000; terminal area chart 1:250,000. Time en route = distance ÷ groundspeed. Fuel burn = time × fuel flow. Always add reserve to planned fuel. Weight and balance: takeoff weight must not exceed max gross; CG must be within envelope at all weights including landing.

tasks.title: National Airspace System
tasks.groundingNotes: Class A: 18,000 ft MSL to FL600, IFR only, ATC clearance required. Class B: surface to 10,000 ft MSL around busiest airports, ATC clearance required, Mode C transponder required within 30 NM. Class C: surface to 4,000 ft AGL, two-way radio contact required, Mode C required. Class D: surface to 2,500 ft AGL, two-way radio contact required. Class E: controlled airspace not A/B/C/D, no clearance required for VFR. Class G: uncontrolled. Special use: Prohibited (no entry), Restricted (need permission), Warning, MOA, Alert, Controlled Firing. TFR: temporary, check NOTAMs. Transponder required: in Class A, B, C; above 10,000 ft MSL (except at or below 2,500 ft AGL); within 30 NM of Class B primary (Mode C veil).

tasks.title: Performance and Limitations
tasks.groundingNotes: Density altitude = pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature. High density altitude reduces: engine power (less dense air = less fuel/air mixture), propeller efficiency, lift. Pressure altitude: set altimeter to 29.92, read altitude. PA = field elevation + (29.92 - local altimeter) × 1000. Performance charts use pressure altitude and temperature. Takeoff distance increases with: high density altitude, high gross weight, tailwind, uphill slope, soft/wet surface. Vx = best angle of climb (obstacle clearance); Vy = best rate of climb (altitude gain); both decrease with altitude. Va (maneuvering speed): do not make full or abrupt control inputs above Va; decreases with lower weight.

tasks.title: Operation of Systems
tasks.groundingNotes: Pitot-static system: altimeter (static), VSI (static), airspeed (pitot + static). Blocked pitot: airspeed reads zero or acts like altimeter. Blocked static: altimeter freezes, VSI freezes, airspeed reads incorrectly. Alternate static source provides slightly different readings (lower static pressure in cockpit). Carburetor ice: most likely 20-70°F OAT with high humidity, at low power settings; symptoms: RPM drop (fixed pitch), MP drop (constant speed); apply carb heat (RPM drops then rises as ice melts). Magnetos: each magneto fires one plug per cylinder; both magnetos on for normal ops; dead mag check: RPM drop of 125 RPM max, differential 50 RPM max. Fuel system: 100LL = blue, Jet-A = clear/straw; always sump tanks and strainer. Electrical: alternator charges battery; ammeter shows charge/discharge rate.

tasks.title: Human Factors
tasks.groundingNotes: IMSAFE checklist: Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol (8 hrs bottle-to-throttle; 0.04 BAC limit per 14 CFR 91.17; 24 hrs recommended), Fatigue, Emotion/Eating. Hypoxia: insufficient oxygen; most dangerous because insidious onset; four types: hypoxic (altitude), hypemic (CO poisoning/anemia), stagnant (heart failure), histotoxic (alcohol/drugs). Hyperventilation: too much CO2 expelled; symptoms similar to hypoxia; remedy: breathe into bag or consciously slow breathing. Spatial disorientation: vestibular system unreliable in IMC; trust instruments. Fatigue: most common human factor in accidents; no legal limit but PIC judgment required. ADM (Aeronautical Decision Making): DECIDE model — Detect, Estimate, Choose, Identify, Do, Evaluate. Hazardous attitudes: antiauthority, impulsivity, invulnerability, macho, resignation — each has an antidote.

areasOfOperation.title: Preflight Procedures
areasOfOperation.groundingNotes: PIC responsible for determining aircraft airworthy before flight (14 CFR 91.7). Preflight follows POH checklist sequence.

tasks.title: Preflight Assessment
tasks.groundingNotes: Fuel: check quantity visually (do not rely on gauges alone for preflight), check color (100LL = blue), sump all tanks and quick-drain for water (water sinks to bottom, appears as clear bubbles). Oil: check level against POH minimums (typically 6-8 qt); check color and consistency. Control surfaces: move through full range, verify correct direction (aileron up on side stick is raised), check for security. Fuel vents: clear of obstructions. Static ports: clear. Pitot tube: clear, pitot cover removed. Tires: proper inflation, no excessive wear or cuts. Lights: all required lights functional. Fuel caps: secure after checking.

tasks.title: Flight Deck Management
tasks.groundingNotes: Seats and seat belts adjusted and locked before engine start. Required documents on board (ARROW). Charts and flight plan accessible. Sterile cockpit concept: minimize distractions during critical phases. Checklist discipline: use actual checklist, not memory alone for abnormal/emergency procedures. Personal electronic devices must not interfere with aircraft systems. Oxygen equipment (if equipped) checked. Loose items secured — become projectiles in turbulence.

tasks.title: Engine Starting
tasks.groundingNotes: Cold start vs. hot start procedures per POH. Prime as needed (carbureted engines). Clear prop before start — call 'Clear prop' and visually verify area clear. Start on correct fuel tank per POH. After start: check oil pressure within 30 seconds (or per POH); ammeter should show positive charge; suction gauge for gyros. Flooded engine: throttle full open, mixture cutoff, crank to clear fuel, then restart per POH. Hot start (fuel-injected): different procedure — consult POH (often involves opening throttle more).

tasks.title: Taxiing
tasks.groundingNotes: Taxi speed: slow enough to stop within half the visible area. Brakes checked early on taxi. Flight controls: headwind = aileron into wind, elevator neutral; tailwind = aileron away from wind, elevator forward (dive away from tailwind). Taxiway markings: yellow centerline, hold short lines (two solid + two dashed = hold here; solid side faces runway). Never cross hold short line without ATC clearance at towered airport. Intersection departures: know runway distance remaining. Runway incursion avoidance: read back all runway crossing and hold short instructions; look before crossing.

tasks.title: Before Takeoff Check
tasks.groundingNotes: Run-up typically at 1800-2000 RPM (per POH). Mag check: max 125 RPM drop single mag, max 50 RPM differential. Carb heat check: RPM drop expected (confirms carb heat is working); return to COLD before takeoff (carb heat reduces power and can introduce unfiltered air). Suction/vacuum gauge: 4.6-5.4 in Hg typical for gyros. Flight controls free and correct direction. Trim set for takeoff. Transponder to ALT. Mixture set per field elevation. Abort criteria: any abnormal instrument reading, rough engine, control issues.

areasOfOperation.title: Airport and Seaplane Base Operations
areasOfOperation.groundingNotes: Standard traffic pattern: left traffic, 1000 ft AGL for piston aircraft, unless otherwise published. CTAF used at non-towered airports.

tasks.title: Communications, Light Signals, and Runway Lighting Systems
tasks.groundingNotes: Light gun signals apply ONLY to towered airports.  Light gun signals — in air: steady green = cleared to land; flashing green = return for landing; steady red = give way, continue circling; flashing red = airport unsafe, do not land; flashing white = return to starting point; alternating red/green = exercise extreme caution. On ground: steady green = cleared for takeoff; flashing green = cleared to taxi; steady red = stop; flashing red = taxi clear of runway; flashing white = return to starting point. ATIS/AWOS/ASOS: obtain before arrival/departure. Non-towered airport: announce position and intentions on CTAF; make a call on each leg of the pattern; listen before transmitting. UNICOM: 122.8 MHz typical. Runway lighting: REIL (runway end identifier lights) = flashing white at threshold; VASI = red over red too low, white over white too high, red over white you're right; PAPI similar but 4 lights. Runway numbers = magnetic heading ÷ 10, rounded.

tasks.title: Traffic Patterns
tasks.groundingNotes: Standard pattern: left traffic; upwind (climb), crosswind (turn 90° to runway), downwind (parallel to runway, opposite direction), base (turn 90° toward runway), final (aligned with runway). Pattern altitude: 1000 ft AGL for piston (800 ft AGL at some airports — check Chart Supplement). Segmented circle: wind sock indicates wind direction; landing strip indicators show runway orientation; traffic pattern indicators show pattern direction. When entering: 45° to midfield downwind preferred. Power-off abeam: reduce power to approach setting. Wake turbulence: land beyond large aircraft's touchdown point; depart before large aircraft's rotation point.

areasOfOperation.title: Takeoffs, Landings, and Go-Arounds
areasOfOperation.groundingNotes: Rotate at Vr (per POH), climb at Vx for obstacles or Vy for altitude gain. Normal landing: approach at 1.3 Vso. Go-around: full power, carb heat off, positive climb, retract flaps incrementally.
areasOfOperation.questionGuidance: De-emphasize Task A (Normal Takeoff and Climb) — it is the least instructionally interesting task in this area and students generally know it well. Weight questions toward Tasks C–H (soft-field, short-field, forward slip, go-around). Prefer comparative questions that highlight how special techniques differ from normal procedures, for example: 'How does a short-field takeoff differ from a normal takeoff?', 'When and why would you use a soft-field technique?', 'What makes a forward slip useful on approach?' If you do ask about normal procedures, connect them to a follow-up that contrasts with a special technique.

tasks.title: Normal Takeoff and Climb
tasks.groundingNotes: Lineup: verify runway heading matches magnetic compass. Apply full power smoothly; check engine instruments in green. Directional control with rudder (not brakes). Rotate at Vr (per POH). After positive rate: retract flaps if extended. Climb at Vy initially; obstacle = Vx. Crosswind correction: crab or slip during climb. Abort criteria: airspeed not alive by ~30 kts, engine roughness, control anomaly.

tasks.title: Normal Approach and Landing
tasks.groundingNotes: Approach speed: 1.3 Vso (per POH). Stabilized approach: on speed, on glide path, on centerline by 200 ft AGL. Flaps: per POH for normal landing. Roundout: begin 10-20 ft AGL, reduce power, raise nose to landing attitude. Flare: hold off as long as possible, touch down at minimum airspeed. Touchdown: main gear first (tricycle gear). After landing: retract flaps, carb heat off, clear runway promptly. Crosswind: side-load on gear — use forward slip or crab-to-slip technique. Maximum demonstrated crosswind is not a limitation but a guideline.

tasks.title: Soft-Field Takeoff and Climb
tasks.groundingNotes: Purpose: minimize time and distance rolling through soft surface (mud, grass, snow). Technique: full back pressure on rollout to transfer weight off nosewheel; lift off at lowest possible airspeed; remain in ground effect (do not climb away); accelerate to Vx or Vy in ground effect before climbing. Do not retract gear while still in ground effect over soft surface. Never stop on soft field — keep moving.

tasks.title: Soft-Field Approach and Landing
tasks.groundingNotes: Purpose: land at minimum speed to avoid nosing over. Technique: normal approach; in flare, hold aircraft off as long as possible; touch down at minimum airspeed with full back pressure maintained; hold back pressure after touchdown to keep weight off nosewheel; use minimal braking. Do not stop on the surface if it can be avoided.

tasks.title: Short-Field Takeoff and Maximum Performance Climb
tasks.groundingNotes: Purpose: clear an obstacle in minimum distance. Technique: position at very beginning of runway; hold brakes; full power; release brakes; rotate at Vr (often same or slightly higher than normal); climb at Vx until obstacle cleared, then accelerate to Vy. Do not rush the rotation — premature rotation increases drag. Flaps: per POH (often 10°).

tasks.title: Short-Field Approach and Landing
tasks.groundingNotes: Purpose: land in minimum distance. Technique: approach at 1.3 Vso (or POH short-field speed, often slightly lower than normal); aim for threshold; touch down at or just past threshold; immediately apply maximum braking; retract flaps to put weight on wheels (increases braking effectiveness). ACS standard: touchdown within 200 ft of specified point.

tasks.title: Forward Slip to a Landing
tasks.groundingNotes: Forward slip: bank into wind (or toward runway) with opposite rudder; aircraft sideslips — increases drag and descent rate without increasing airspeed. Use: lose excess altitude on final without increasing airspeed. NOT the same as a crosswind slip (which aligns ground track). Technique: bank one wing down, apply opposite rudder to prevent turn; maintain runway alignment; return to wings-level before touchdown. Airspeed indicator may be unreliable in a slip (pitot not aligned with airflow). Do not slip with full flaps on some aircraft (check POH — can cause tail blanking).

tasks.title: Go-Around / Rejected Landing
tasks.groundingNotes: Decision: make early — any doubt = go around. Never hesitate once committed. Technique: full power immediately; carb heat OFF (reduces power); establish positive climb attitude; retract flaps incrementally to recommended go-around setting (not all at once — sudden lift loss); retract remaining flaps after positive climb established; clear obstacle/runway. Common error: reducing flaps too quickly causing aircraft to sink. Announce intentions on CTAF/tower. Go-around is always the right decision.

areasOfOperation.title: Performance and Ground Reference Maneuvers
areasOfOperation.groundingNotes: Load factor at 45° bank = 1.41G. Ground reference maneuvers correct for wind drift by varying bank — steeper downwind, shallower upwind.

tasks.title: Steep Turns
tasks.groundingNotes: Bank angle: 45°. Standards: altitude ±100 ft, airspeed ±10 kt, heading ±10° on rollout. Technique: add back pressure AND slight power as bank increases to maintain both altitude and airspeed. Be aware that load factor increases stall speed. Left turn vs. right turn: torque/P-factor cause left-turning tendency — right turn requires more rudder. Overbanking tendency: in steep turn, outer wing moves faster and generates more lift — tendency to steepen bank; correct with slight opposite aileron. Common error: losing altitude (not adding enough back pressure).

tasks.title: Ground Reference Maneuvers
tasks.groundingNotes: Turns around a point: maintain constant radius; steeper bank downwind (highest groundspeed), shallower upwind (lowest groundspeed). Entry: downwind, 45° abeam reference point. S-turns across a road: symmetrical half-circles; steepest bank immediately after crossing road going downwind; shallowest at top of circle going upwind. Rectangular course: simulates traffic pattern; correct for wind on each leg. All maneuvers: altitude 600-1000 ft AGL, entry speed at or below Va, two 90° clearing turns before maneuver.

areasOfOperation.title: Navigation
areasOfOperation.groundingNotes: True course → magnetic course (±variation) → magnetic heading (±deviation). If lost: climb, conserve, communicate, confess, comply.

tasks.title: Pilotage and Dead Reckoning
tasks.groundingNotes: Pilotage: match visual landmarks to sectional chart. Dead reckoning: use heading, airspeed, wind correction angle, and time. Wind correction angle (WCA): crab into wind to maintain course. Time en route = distance ÷ groundspeed. Estimated groundspeed: true airspeed ± wind component. Variation: East = subtract from true to get magnetic; West = add (East is least, West is best). Sectional chart scale: 1:500,000. METAR/TAF altimeter setting: use current local setting (QNH). Checkpoint passage: note time, compare to planned, update ETA. If correcting for wind other than forecast, correct to adjust the WCA plus more correction to get back on course.

tasks.title: Navigation Systems and Radar Services
tasks.groundingNotes: VOR: CDI centers when on selected radial; TO = flying toward station (or would intercept flying to); FROM = flying away. Full-scale deflection = 10°, 2° per dot (5-dot display). Reverse sensing: flying FROM with TO selected — turn away from needle. Radar services: flight following (VFR advisory service from ATC, workload permitting); transponder required (Mode C preferred). GPS: supplemental for VFR; RAIM required for IFR. ATC communication: squawk code assigned; ident when requested; altitude readout from Mode C.

tasks.title: Diversion
tasks.groundingNotes: When to divert: weather, fuel, emergency, aircraft problem. Decision: make early — don't wait until critical. Technique: identify alternate on chart; estimate heading (rough magnetic course); estimate time (distance ÷ estimated groundspeed); estimate fuel required; advise ATC if in contact. Mental math shortcuts: 60:1 rule — 1° off per 60 NM = 1 NM off course. Declare emergency if needed (no penalty for precautionary declaration). Contact FSS or approach for assistance.

tasks.title: Lost Procedures
tasks.groundingNotes: Climb: increases radio range, improves visibility for landmarks. Conserve fuel: reduce power. Communicate: contact ATC (121.5 emergency frequency, or last assigned frequency). Confess: tell ATC you are lost. Comply: follow ATC instructions. Transponder: squawk 7700 (emergency) if appropriate. Radar vectors: ATC can identify and vector if equipped with transponder. Do not press on into deteriorating weather while lost — land at nearest suitable airport.

areasOfOperation.title: Slow Flight and Stalls
areasOfOperation.groundingNotes: LEFT-TURNING TENDENCIES require RIGHT RUDDER — this applies to ALL tasks in this area. Four causes: torque, P-factor, spiraling slipstream, gyroscopic precession. All cause left yaw; all corrected with right rudder.

tasks.title: Maneuvering During Slow Flight
tasks.groundingNotes: Slow flight: at minimum controllable airspeed (just above stall, with stall warning active per ACS). Power: high power required to maintain altitude at low airspeed (in region of reversed command — more drag requires more power, not less). Controls: sloppy, mushy response; increased right rudder required to counteract torque/P-factor at high power low airspeed. Stall warning: should be on throughout slow flight exercise. If stall warning stops: too slow (on back side of power curve). Configuration: flaps as specified; gear as specified. ACS standard: ±10 kt airspeed, ±10° heading, ±100 ft altitude.

tasks.title: Power-Off Stalls
tasks.groundingNotes: Simulate approach-to-landing stall (configuration: gear down if applicable, landing flaps). Setup: reduce power to idle, maintain altitude (increasing back pressure as speed decreases). At stall: buffet, break, possible roll/yaw. Recovery: simultaneously — reduce back pressure (reduce AOA), add full power, apply right rudder for coordination; retract flaps incrementally; maintain coordinated flight. Do NOT roll wings level before reducing AOA. Secondary stall: occurs if back pressure reapplied before airspeed builds — avoid. ACS: recover with minimum altitude loss.

tasks.title: Power-On Stalls
tasks.groundingNotes: Simulate takeoff/departure stall (configuration: flaps as for takeoff or clean). Setup: from level flight, reduce airspeed to rotation speed, apply takeoff power, pitch up aggressively. Left roll/yaw tendency at stall is more pronounced than power-off — right rudder CRITICAL. At stall: break or buffet. Recovery: reduce back pressure first (even slight), then hold power (or add if not already at max), right rudder, coordinate. Do NOT pull back harder or add left aileron at stall — both worsen situation. ACS: recover with minimum altitude loss.

tasks.title: Spin Awareness
tasks.groundingNotes: Spin entry requires BOTH stall AND yaw (uncoordinated flight). Inside (pro-spin) skid more dangerous than outside slip. PARE recovery: Power off, Ailerons neutral, Rudder full opposite to rotation (this is primary recovery control in a spin — ailerons are ineffective or adverse), Elevator forward to break stall. Incipient spin: first 1-2 turns, rotation not yet stabilized — most recoverable. Developed spin: stabilized rotation, requires full PARE. Spin vs. spiral dive: spiral — increasing airspeed, high G, wings appear level from inside; spin — low/stable airspeed, low G, clear rotation. Recovery from spiral dive: reduce power first, level wings, THEN pull — opposite sequence from spin.

areasOfOperation.title: Basic Instrument Maneuvers
areasOfOperation.groundingNotes: Attitude indicator is the primary control instrument. Altimeter, VSI, airspeed are performance instruments. Scan centered on AI.

tasks.title: Straight-and-Level Flight
tasks.groundingNotes: Instrument cross-check: attitude indicator (pitch and bank), altimeter (pitch result), VSI (pitch trend), airspeed (pitch/power), heading indicator (bank result). Lag instruments: altimeter and VSI lag behind actual altitude change — lead with AI. Trim: use trim to relieve control pressure, not to fly the aircraft. Common error: fixating on one instrument.

tasks.title: Constant Airspeed Climbs
tasks.groundingNotes: Establish climb: add power, raise pitch on AI, trim. Monitor: AI (pitch), airspeed (target climb speed), VSI (positive trend), heading (wings level). Level off: begin reducing pitch at ~10% of climb rate before target altitude (e.g., 500 fpm climb = start leveling 50 ft early). Accelerate to cruise, reduce power, trim.

tasks.title: Constant Airspeed Descents
tasks.groundingNotes: Establish descent: reduce power, lower pitch on AI, trim. Power-off descent: add carb heat before reducing power. Monitor: AI (pitch), airspeed (target descent speed), VSI (negative trend), heading. Level off: add power, raise pitch, trim. Common error: chasing the altimeter instead of flying the AI.

tasks.title: Turns to Headings
tasks.groundingNotes: Standard rate turn: 3°/second; bank angle ≈ (airspeed ÷ 10) + 7. Time for turns: 180° = 60 sec, 90° = 30 sec, 360° = 120 sec at standard rate. Lead the rollout: start rolling out ~5-10° before target heading (half the bank angle). Heading indicator: set to compass before flight; re-check every 15 minutes (precesses). Magnetic compass errors in turns: UNOS (Undershoot North, Overshoot South) in northern hemisphere.

tasks.title: Recovery from Unusual Flight Attitudes
tasks.groundingNotes: Nose-high unusual attitude: increase power, lower nose on AI, level wings — order matters (power first prevents stall). Nose-low unusual attitude: reduce power first (prevents overspeed), level wings, raise nose — NEVER pull back with wings banked (increases G and tightens spiral). ANDS acceleration error on compass: Accelerate North, Decelerate South (in northern hemisphere). UNOS turning error: Undershoot North, Overshoot South.

tasks.title: Radio Communications, Navigation Systems/Facilities, and Radar Services
tasks.groundingNotes: VOR under the hood: identify station (morse code), set OBS, interpret CDI. Intercepting a radial: determine position, calculate intercept heading (45° is standard). Tracking a radial: center needle, correct for wind drift. Holding: standard = right turns, 1-minute legs below 14,000 ft. Lost comms: squawk 7600; in VMC remain VMC and land as soon as practicable; in IMC — AVEF (Assigned, Vectored, Expected, Filed altitude; fly most direct route; begin approach at EFC or ETA).

areasOfOperation.title: Emergency Operations
areasOfOperation.groundingNotes: Squawk 7700 emergency, 7600 lost comms, 7500 hijack. ELT: 406 MHz digital + 121.5 MHz analog.

tasks.title: Emergency Descent
tasks.groundingNotes: When used: onboard fire, rapid decompression (high altitude), smoke in cockpit. Technique: reduce power to idle; extend gear (if retractable — adds drag, limits speed); bank 30-45° (increases descent rate, maintains positive G); maintain Vne or Vmo minus margin (per POH); descend as rapidly as possible. Declare emergency: squawk 7700, contact ATC. Don oxygen if available. Do NOT dive vertically — structural limits.

tasks.title: Emergency Approach and Landing
tasks.groundingNotes: Engine failure: best glide speed (Vg) from POH immediately — do not delay. Memory items: airspeed to Vg, identify best field, checklist (fuel selector, mixture, ignition, carb heat, primer). Glide ratio: in most trainers approximately 8:1 (8 ft forward per 1 ft down). Field selection: length, surface, slope, obstacles, wind. Forced landing: ABCDE — Airspeed (Vg), Best field, Checklist, Declare/Distress, Execute. Mayday call: 'Mayday mayday mayday, [aircraft ID], [position], [altitude], [nature of emergency], [souls on board], [fuel remaining].' High key/low key pattern: high key = abeam field at 1000 ft AGL; low key = base at 500 ft AGL (general guidelines). Carb heat: apply early in power reduction — prevents carb ice from developing.

tasks.title: Systems and Equipment Malfunctions
tasks.groundingNotes: Electrical failure: master off (if non-essential) to conserve battery; use battery sparingly for radio/transponder; land as soon as practicable. Electrical fire: master off, vents open, extinguisher if available, land immediately. Engine fire on ground: mixture cutoff, fuel off, ignition off, evacuate. Engine fire in flight: fuel off, mixture cutoff, heat off, maintain glide, land ASAP. Pitot blockage (ice): airspeed reads zero or acts like altimeter (reads higher as altitude increases); activate pitot heat. Static blockage: altimeter freezes, VSI freezes, airspeed error; use alternate static (slight reading differences expected). Vacuum failure: AI and HI fail; use partial panel (turn coordinator + compass).

tasks.title: Emergency Equipment and Survival Gear
tasks.groundingNotes: ELT: required for most aircraft (14 CFR 91.207); activates on impact (>5G); transmits on 406 MHz (registered with NOAA) and 121.5 MHz; battery must be replaced/recharged at 50% used or expiration date. Fire extinguisher: Halon or CO2 preferred (non-conductive); aim at base of fire. First aid kit: not required by regulation but recommended. Life preservers: required over water more than 50 NM from shore or beyond gliding distance. Survival gear for remote areas: required when operating over unpopulated areas per 14 CFR 91.509 (large/turbine); best practice for all operations over remote terrain.

tasks.title: Aeromedical Factors and Physiology
tasks.groundingNotes: Hypoxia: insufficient oxygen at cells; hypoxic type begins above 10,000 ft (night) or 12,500 ft (day); symptoms: euphoria, impaired judgment, cyanosis; treatment: supplemental O2, descend. Hyperventilation: blow off CO2; symptoms mimic hypoxia; treatment: slow breathing, breathe into bag, talk/sing. Spatial disorientation: vestibular system unreliable in IMC; leans, graveyard spiral illusion; prevention: trust instruments. Vision: cones (color, day, central), rods (night, peripheral); averted vision at night (look 10° off target); dark adaptation 30 minutes, destroyed instantly by bright light. Carbon monoxide: colorless, odorless; from heater exhaust; CO detector recommended; treatment: fresh air, 100% O2, land immediately. Alcohol: impairs judgment; 8-hour rule minimum; 0.04 BAC limit; avoid if hungover (residual effects).

areasOfOperation.title: Night Operations
areasOfOperation.groundingNotes: Dark adaptation: 30 minutes; destroyed by bright light. Averted vision: look 10° off target to use rods. Anti-collision lights required sunset to sunrise.

tasks.title: Night Preparation
tasks.groundingNotes: Currency: 3 full-stop landings within 90 days after sunset to carry passengers; must be full-stop (not touch-and-go) (14 CFR 61.57(b)). Night defined as: end of evening civil twilight to beginning of morning civil twilight (for logging); 1 hour after sunset to 1 hour before sunrise (for passenger currency). Required equipment for night VFR (14 CFR 91.205(c)): all day VFR instruments plus position lights, anti-collision light, landing light (for hire), spare fuses. Position lights: red = left wingtip, green = right wingtip, white = tail. Remember: aircraft coming toward you — red on left, green on right (like a ship). Illusions: autokinesis (stationary light appears to move — fixate on multiple references), black hole approach (no terrain/cultural lighting — leads to low approach), false horizon (sloped cloud layer or terrain). Preflight: flashlight, extra batteries; all lights tested; cockpit lighting adjusted to preserve night vision.

areasOfOperation.title: Postflight Procedures
areasOfOperation.groundingNotes: Secure aircraft per POH. Log discrepancies in maintenance logbook.

tasks.title: After Landing, Parking, and Securing
tasks.groundingNotes: After landing checklist: flaps up, carb heat off, transponder to standby or ALT, taxi clear of runway before stopping. Shutdown: mixture to idle cutoff (fuel-injected: cutoff first, then ignition off when engine stops from fuel starvation); magnetos off; master off; control lock installed. Securing: chocks placed; tie-downs on all three points; pitot cover installed; fuel to required level for next flight; oil checked; squawk any discrepancies in aircraft maintenance logbook (write-up is legal requirement for known defects). Hobbs vs. tach time: Hobbs runs with master on; tach runs proportional to RPM — used for maintenance intervals.
